title: Chapter 11: General Properties of Waves — Medium

questions.question: A rope wave is traveling horizontally from left to right. In which direction do the individual particles of the rope move?
options: Along with the wave to the right
questions.explanation:
Rope waves are transverse; while the wave (energy) travels sideways, the rope particles themselves only move up and down. 

**Common mistake:** Believing the particles move in the same direction as the wave (longitudinal behavior).

questions.question: What does a frequency of $2\text{ Hz}$ mean for a vibrating source?
options: It makes two complete oscillations per second
It takes two seconds to complete one oscillation
The wave travels two metres every second
The wave has a height of two centimetres
questions.explanation:
Frequency is the number of complete waves or oscillations produced per unit time; $2\text{ Hz}$ means two cycles every second. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing frequency with period, which is the time for one oscillation (0.5 s in this case).

questions.question: Compare the motion of particles in a longitudinal wave and a transverse wave. Which statement is correct?
options: Both involve oscillations, but the directions relative to the wave travel are different.
Longitudinal particles move in circles; transverse particles move in straight lines.
Transverse waves require a medium, but longitudinal waves do not.
Only transverse waves can transfer energy without matter.
questions.explanation:
Both wave types involve periodic oscillations that transfer energy; the distinction is whether the oscillation is parallel (longitudinal) or perpendicular (transverse) to the wave direction. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking one type is 'better' at transferring energy than the other.

questions.question: A slinky is stretched out on a floor. To produce a longitudinal pulse, in which direction should the student move the end of the slinky?
options: Forward and backward along the line of the slinky
Side to side at a right angle to the slinky
Up and down away from the floor
questions.explanation:
To create a longitudinal wave, the vibration must be parallel to the direction of propagation, which is achieved by moving the end forward and backward. 

**Common mistake:** Moving it side-to-side, which would produce a transverse wave.

questions.question: How is energy transferred from a moving hand (source) to a rope particle further away?
options: Kinetic energy is passed between adjacent rope particles as the wave progresses.
The hand throws rope molecules toward the far end.
The energy is converted into magnetic potential energy in the rope.
Air molecules carry the energy alongside the rope.
questions.explanation:
According to the notes, the kinetic energy from the source is transferred to rope particles near it, which then pass it to particles further away as the wave progresses. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the energy travels independently of the particles.

questions.question: A wave source has a period of $0.05\text{ s}$. What is its frequency?
questions.explanation:
Frequency $f = 1/T = 1 / 0.05 = 20\text{ Hz}$. 

**Common mistake:** Multiplying by the period instead of taking the reciprocal.

questions.question: A displacement-distance graph shows that the distance between the first crest and the third crest of a wave is $10\text{ cm}$. What is the wavelength?
questions.explanation:
The distance from the 1st to the 3rd crest covers two full wavelengths ($2\lambda$). Therefore, $\lambda = 10 / 2 = 5\text{ cm}$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking the distance between the 1st and 3rd crest is just one wavelength.

questions.question: Water waves enter a dock at a rate of $120$ crests per minute. Calculate the frequency of these waves in hertz (Hz).
questions.explanation:
Frequency is waves per 'second'. $120$ crests per minute $= 120 / 60 = 2\text{ Hz}$. 

**Common mistake:** Using the 'per minute' value directly as the frequency in Hz.

questions.question: A wave travels at $0.50\text{ m/s}$ with a wavelength of $5\text{ cm}$. Calculate its frequency.
questions.explanation:
First, convert $\lambda$ to metres: $5\text{ cm} = 0.05\text{ m}$. Then $f = v / \lambda = 0.50 / 0.05 = 10\text{ Hz}$. 

**Common mistake:** Failing to convert cm to m, leading to $0.50 / 5 = 0.1\text{ Hz}$.

questions.question: Two waves, P and Q, travel at the same speed. If the frequency of wave P is twice that of wave Q, how do their wavelengths compare?
options: Wavelength of P is half that of Q
Wavelength of P is twice that of Q
$Wavelength of P is four \times that of Q$
questions.explanation:
Since $v = f\lambda$, for a constant speed, frequency and wavelength are inversely proportional. Doubling the frequency results in halving the wavelength. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking frequency and wavelength are directly proportional.

questions.question: A student narrows the gap in a barrier in a ripple tank. What effect does this have on the diffraction of the water waves?
questions.explanation:
As the gap size decreases relative to the wavelength, the curvature of the diffracted wavefronts increases, meaning the waves spread out more.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Water waves in a deep region have a wavelength of $4.0\text{ cm}$. They enter a shallow region where their speed is halved. What is the new wavelength?
questions.explanation:
Using $v = f\lambda$, since frequency $f$ is constant, speed $v$ is directly proportional to wavelength $\lambda$. If speed is halved, wavelength is also halved ($4.0 / 2 = 2.0\text{ cm}$).

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Which diagram correctly describes waves passing from deep to shallow water?
options: Wavefronts move closer together in shallow water
Wavefronts move further apart in shallow water
Wavefronts bend away from the normal
questions.explanation:
Waves travel slower in shallow water. Since $v = f\lambda$ and $f$ is constant, the wavelength $\lambda$ (the distance between wavefronts) must decrease.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: Waves approach a single edge. Which condition results in the greatest amount of diffraction (curving around the edge)?
questions.explanation:
For diffraction at an edge, a longer wavelength causes a greater curvature effect, allowing more of the wave to spread behind the barrier.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.

questions.question: What occurs when plane waves strike a straight barrier at an angle of incidence of $0^circ$?
options: The waves reflect back along their original path
The waves are refracted towards the normal
The wavelength of the reflected waves doubles
questions.explanation:
An angle of incidence of $0^circ$ means the waves hit the barrier head-on. They reflect directly back. Refraction and diffraction require boundaries between media or gaps/edges.

**Common mistake:** Review the method above and check units/signs.
